statusLine: EU fuel reserves sit at watch-level overall, with 14 of 27 member states holding at least one fuel type below the mandatory 90-day minimum and jet fuel emerging as a system-wide vulnerability.
fullAnalysis:
As of June 2026, the EU's aggregate oil stock position remains under strain following recent strategic reserve releases tied to Middle East supply disruptions. EU-wide averages stand at 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87 days for jet fuel — meaning the bloc's diesel and jet fuel averages are themselves below the mandatory 90-day net import minimum. While the petrol average provides a modest buffer, these headline figures mask significant divergence across member states, with smaller and landlocked economies facing the sharpest shortfalls.

Jet fuel is the most consistently stressed product across the dataset. Of the 27 countries surveyed, 19 hold jet fuel stocks below the 90-day threshold, with several registering critically low figures: Portugal at 21.2 days, Poland at 22.5 days, and Austria and Finland both at 31.9 days. This pattern suggests aviation supply chains are bearing a disproportionate share of the drawdown from strategic reserve releases and elevated demand. Only Ireland, Estonia, Slovenia, Croatia, and Sweden report jet fuel positions at or comfortably above the minimum. Diesel reserves tell a similar story, with 16 of 27 countries below 90 days, including large economies such as Italy (61.4 days), Spain (64.4 days), and France (89.3 days — just under the threshold).

At the country level, Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal stand out as carrying critical-status ratings across all three fuel types simultaneously. Romania's diesel stocks at 30.2 days represent the lowest single diesel reading in the dataset. Hungary, Bulgaria, Latvia, Estonia, Ireland, and Denmark present a contrasting picture, with generally strong petrol and diesel positions, though Denmark's jet fuel at 75.7 days and Latvia's at 51.6 days illustrate that even better-positioned states carry specific product gaps. Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February 2026, moving from $72.48 to $80.59 per barrel, which is increasing restocking costs at precisely the moment several member states need to rebuild depleted reserves.

Fuel pump prices as of mid-June reflect a wide spread across the bloc, with petrol ranging from €1.34 per litre in Malta to €2.39 in Denmark — a gap largely attributable to differing national tax structures rather than supply costs alone. The EU petrol average of €1.771 per litre and diesel average of €1.781 per litre indicate that crude price increases have begun passing through to consumers, and any further rise in Brent would add further pressure on household and commercial fuel budgets, particularly in countries where stocks are already tight and import dependency is high.
keyPoints: EU diesel and jet fuel averages (88.1 days and 87 days respectively) are both below the mandatory 90-day minimum, placing the bloc's overall status at watch level as of March 2026 stock data.

Jet fuel is the most widespread vulnerability: 19 of 27 member states hold jet fuel stocks below the 90-day threshold, with Portugal (21.2 days), Poland (22.5 days), and Austria (31.9 days) among the lowest.

Five member states — Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal — carry critical-status ratings across all three fuel types simultaneously, with Romania's diesel stocks at just 30.2 days being the lowest single-product reading in the dataset.

Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February 2026 to $80.59 per barrel, raising restocking costs at a time when multiple member states are actively drawing down or need to replenish strategic reserves.

A wide petrol price spread — from €1.34 per litre in Malta to €2.39 in Denmark — reflects divergent national tax regimes, but further crude price increases risk compounding affordability pressures in countries already facing stock shortfalls.
